question.text: Under Regulation 7, what must happen to partly paid-up shares of an issuer before filing an IPO offer document?

answer.text: They must be disclosed in the offer document as a risk factor
They must be converted into preference shares
They must be either fully paid up or forfeited
They must be held in an escrow account until listing

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Regulation 7 requires that all partly paid-up shares must be either fully paid up or forfeited before the issuer can file its offer document. This ensures a clean capital structure with no ambiguity about paid-up capital at the time of the public offer. **The Trap:** Simply disclosing partly paid shares as a risk factor is insufficient — SEBI requires their resolution (full payment or forfeiture) before the offer document is filed. This is a mandatory pre-condition, not just a disclosure requirement. **Cross-Exam Context:** Clean capital structure as a pre-condition for public offers is a principle also relevant in NISM Series VIII when understanding shareholding patterns of listed companies. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** During the initial structuring phase, conduct a share capital audit to identify any partly paid-up shares. Initiate the call for payment or forfeiture proceedings well in advance, as these processes require board resolutions and shareholder communications that take time.
